summary:
Ukiyo-e (Pictures of the Floating World, c. 1670–1900 AD) was the distinctive Japanese art form of the [[event:2376|Edo period]] — woodblock prints and paintings depicting the pleasures and entertainments of urban merchant life (kabuki actors, sumo wrestlers, courtesans, landscapes, and seasonal scenes) that became one of history's most influential artistic movements, directly inspiring the Impressionist and Post-Impressionist revolution in European art. The 'floating world' (ukiyo) was the transient realm of pleasure that the merchant classes of Edo, Osaka, and Kyoto inhabited — theaters, teahouses, and brothels — and ukiyo-e art depicted this world with a vivid, dynamic aesthetic that combined bold outlines, flat areas of color, and asymmetrical compositions derived from Chinese ink painting.

Ukiyo-e's technical innovation was the multi-color woodblock print (nishiki-e), developed around 1765, which allowed relatively affordable mass reproduction of sophisticated colored images — making art accessible to a broad urban public in a way previously impossible. Major masters include Hokusai (whose Great Wave off Kanagawa remains among the world's most recognized images), Hiroshige (landscape prints that influenced Van Gogh, Monet, and Degas), and Utamaro (known for sensual bijin-ga, 'pictures of beautiful women'). When Japan opened to the West after 1853, ukiyo-e prints — cheap, lightweight, often used as packing paper — flooded European markets, triggering 'Japonisme' — a craze for Japanese aesthetics that transformed Western art. Van Gogh copied ukiyo-e prints directly, and their influence on the development of modern Western art is now well-documented.
